title: The Evening Brief: Iran strikes reach 11th night, nuclear site threatened
dek: The US struck Iran for an 11th consecutive night on July 22, and Trump named an underground nuclear site as a possible next target, while Lebanon, Ukraine, Nicaragua and trade all moved on separate tracks.
author: GoCheckMyNews
key_fact: The Iran campaign has moved from military and shipping targets toward an explicit threat against nuclear infrastructure, a step Iran's military has said it would answer broadly.
bottom_line: The US struck Iran for an 11th consecutive night on July 22, and President Trump named an underground site near Natanz, referred to in reporting as Pickaxe Mountain, as a possible next target, prompting an Iranian warning of broader retaliation. Alongside that, Lebanon's army moved into a pilot zone vacated by Israel, Ukraine replaced both its defence minister and top general within days, Nicaragua's Ortega declared an end to elections, and Trump imposed 50 percent tariffs on Canadian goods effective in 30 days. Britain's public finances showed a better-than-forecast June borrowing figure but a worse-than-forecast year-to-date position ahead of the autumn Budget. The common thread is negotiated arrangements and command structures under active stress, with the Iran campaign's next steps the most consequential unknown. Watch whether the threatened strike on the nuclear site occurs, how Iran responds, the Lebanon pilot zone's stability, the Canada tariff window, and Britain's autumn Budget.
body: The day's dominant story is the pace and direction of the US strike campaign against Iran. Stories filed July 21 described a 10th consecutive night of strikes on July 20, following an Iranian attack on the Muwaffaq Salti base in Jordan that killed three US service members. By July 22, the campaign had extended to an 11th consecutive night, with US Central Command reporting strikes on military operations centres, maritime capabilities, aircraft hangars, drone storage and logistics infrastructure, and explosions reported in Tabriz, Chabahar, Konarak and Bushehr. President Trump said the next target could be an underground site near Natanz that reporting refers to as Pickaxe Mountain, suspected of housing an undeclared uranium enrichment facility. He said 'We'll be hitting that area pretty soon, and very heavily.' Iran's military command warned it would retaliate 'on all the interests of America, the allies and supporters' if the site is struck.

Elsewhere, several long-running arrangements showed signs of strain or renegotiation. In Lebanon, army units entered the pilot zone villages of Zawtar al-Gharbiyeh, Froun and Srifa on Tuesday, the first concrete step under the US-brokered Trilateral Framework following Israeli withdrawal, though an Israeli source told Haaretz that Israeli troops were not present in two of the three villages to begin with. In Ukraine, President Zelensky removed commander-in-chief Oleksandr Syrskyi, 60, and replaced him with Maj Gen Mykhailo Drapatyi, 43, days after firing Defence Minister Mykhailo Fedorov amid street protests; Yevhenii Khmara replaces Fedorov. In Nicaragua, President Daniel Ortega declared in a Sunday speech marking the Sandinista Revolution's anniversary that 'There won't be anymore elections,' formally ending the pretense of electoral competition ahead of a vote that had been scheduled for November 2027.

On trade and economics, President Trump imposed 50 percent tariffs on a broad range of Canadian goods, including wine, hockey sticks and cement, effective in 30 days, while exempting energy, potash, critical minerals and fish. Prime Minister Mark Carney said Canada stands ready to intensify negotiations; Ontario Premier Doug Ford called for retaliatory tariffs. In Britain, June borrowing came in at 16 billion pounds, below the Office for Budget Responsibility's forecast, but year-to-date borrowing is running 2.7 billion pounds above OBR projections, a gap that constrains the government's room to maneuver ahead of the autumn Budget.

Other developments included France's parliament passing a law banning social media for under-15s, with new-account restrictions from September 2026 and full age verification from January 2027; four former Southern Water executives, including ex-CEO Matthew Wright, charged with conspiracy to defraud environmental regulators after a High Court judge dismissed Wright's challenge to the case; a Johns Hopkins phase 1 trial reporting that an experimental pancreatic cancer vaccine triggered immune responses in 18 of 20 high-risk participants; and reporting that Rep. Cory Mills faces an active but undisclosed DOJ criminal investigation alongside a House Ethics probe. The Trump administration is also expected to announce a 30-year nuclear cooperation agreement with Saudi Arabia permitting uranium enrichment on Saudi soil, a provision that has drawn concern from Israeli officials and some US lawmakers.

Why these events are unfolding together is only partly answered by the reporting. The Iran strikes are described as retaliation for the deaths of US service members; the collapse of the prior ceasefire framework that preceded the 11th night of strikes is not detailed in the record available. Ukraine's leadership change followed public protests and a confirmed conflict between the fired defence minister and dismissed general, though the reporting does not say what prompted that conflict. Nicaragua's declaration and the Southern Water charges rest on their own separate timelines with no stated connection to the other stories.
No desk boards were available this cycle to check current figures against the day's reporting.

In the coming days, watch whether the US follows through on striking the Natanz-area site and how Iran responds if it does; whether the Lebanese army's presence in the pilot zone holds without incident from Hezbollah or a further Israeli pullback; the 30-day window before US tariffs on Canada take effect and whether talks with Carney's government produce any change; Drapatyi's first operational decisions in Ukraine; the UK's autumn Budget, where the borrowing gap will be tested against government promises; and the scheduled proceedings in the Southern Water fraud case at Medway Magistrates' Court.
